content:
{
  "title": "Fact-Anchor Prompting: Explicit Fact Attribution to Reduce Hallucination in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models often generate plausible but incorrect information, known as hallucinations, especially in open-ended generation tasks. Existing prompting methods do not explicitly require attribution to source facts, leading to weak factual grounding.",
  "motivation": "Hallucinations can degrade user trust and limit the adoption of LLMs in critical applications such as healthcare or legal advice. As LLMs become more widely used, addressing factuality through prompt design—without retraining—offers a scalable and modular solution.",
  "method": "We propose Fact-Anchor Prompting, where prompts are augmented with instructions to explicitly attribute each generated statement to a supporting fact, either provided in-context or retrieved on-the-fly via tool use. Before answering, the LLM must first enumerate 'fact-anchors' (i.e., cited snippets or knowledge triples) and then generate answers constrained to these anchors, referencing them numerically. This is enforced via prompt structure and example completions, and can be extended to tool-augmented scenarios where the model retrieves facts from external databases when insufficient evidence is present. The method aims to condition model output on verifiable facts and make hallucinations more detectable.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate on knowledge-intensive QA datasets such as HotpotQA, Natural Questions, and TruthfulQA. Baselines include standard zero-shot and chain-of-thought prompting. Metrics include factuality (via human and automatic fact-checking), hallucination rate, and answer helpfulness. Ablations test the impact of anchor enumeration and external retrieval."
}
